agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider 2025 Homeless Count results for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council received the 2025 Homeless Count report on April 14, 2026. The count identified 2,715 homeless individuals on March 11, 2025 (1,952 sheltered, 763 unsheltered), a 12% increase from 2023. 79% of respondents were homeless for over a year. 42% identified as Indigenous (18 times more likely than non-Indigenous). 83% were on income assistance or PWD. 42% of housing losses related to eviction.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver 2026 Cultural Grants for operating and spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving $5,509,770 in 2026 Cultural Grants to 85 arts and culture organizations. This includes $2,828,160 in operating grants to 5 institutions, $709,760 in annual grants to 26 organizations, and $1,897,550 per year for three years (2026–2028) to 52 multi-year grantees. Also proposed: a $75,000 grant to Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society for Phase II site assessment, and a $728,000 one-time grant to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades. Recommendations A–C, E, and G require a two-thirds council vote under Vancouver Charter section 206(1).
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from businesses; this is a council vote on grant approvals.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026 Cultural Grants

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on approving 19 community sport event grants totaling $89,000 for organizations listed in the staff report. Grants range from $1,000 to $5,000 per event. The program budget is $244,000 for 2026, with a second intake in Fall 2026. Approval requires a two-thirds council vote under Section 206(1)(i) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a non-profit community sport organization in Vancouver, watch for the council vote on April 14, 2026 — this grant could fund your event.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver 2026 property tax levy distribution
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a bylaw to set the 2026 property tax levy distribution at approximately 57.5% residential and 42.5% non-residential, with no tax shift proposed. The total levy is $1.22 billion with a 0% tax increase. Tax rates for Light industry (Class 5) and Business/other (Class 6) will be calculated on a blended basis, and targeted 5-year land assessment averaging continues for Residential (Class 1), Light industry (Class 5), and Business/other (Class 6) properties.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — this directly affects your 2026 property tax bill.

key_dates.label: Council decision on 2026 property tax levy distribution

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive a report on 2025 property tax exemptions. In 2025, $60.9 billion of properties (12% of the Assessment Roll) were exempt, resulting in $335.1 million in forgone general purpose taxes, partially offset by $34.8 million in compensating payments-in-lieu of taxes. Any net forgone taxes are redistributed to other taxpayers. The report lists statutory exemptions (e.g., charities, churches, hospitals) and permissive exemptions Council may enact by by-law. No new permissive exemptions for not-for-profit properties have been implemented.

key_dates.label: Council meeting to receive 2025 property tax exemptions report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a new Coastal Flood Management Policy that would set rules for how the city prepares for rising sea levels and storm surges. If approved, this policy could affect property owners, developers, and businesses in low-lying areas near the coast. The proposed policy would require new buildings and major renovations to meet a minimum flood construction level of 4.6m (incorporating 1m of sea level rise by 2100), and it could also change how the city manages parks, roads, and other public land along the shoreline. Council is set to vote on this proposal on April 14, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — this policy could affect your property or business if you are in a coastal flood-risk area.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Coastal Flood Management Policy

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver council to vote on public plaza drinking sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposed program allowing alcohol consumption in 13 public plazas from 2026 to 2029, plus a 2-year extension for the Drinker's Lounge parklet at 111 Princess St. The program would shift to a 3-year cycle. Permissible hours align with washroom operating hours. This is not yet approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — if approved, check if your business is near a designated plaza and review liquor licence implications.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver seeks provincial grant to speed up development approvals
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on applying for a $200,000 grant from the BC Local Government Development Approvals Program to redesign approval processes for large-scale housing developments. The project aims to simplify regulations, create reusable guides and templates, and reduce delays for higher-density and mixed-use projects. The application was submitted March 13, 2026; council endorsement is required before a contribution agreement can be signed.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No immediate action needed. Watch for council's decision on April 14, 2026 — if approved, faster approvals could benefit your high-density development projects.

key_dates.label: Council vote on LGDAP grant application

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on 11 housing rezoning condition changes for specific Vancouver properties, implementing the Rental Development Relief Program (RDRP). The two-year pilot (Feb 1, 2026 – Dec 15, 2027) allows eligible mid/high-rise BMR projects to adjust affordability from a 20% discount to CMHC city-wide average rents, up to rates not exceeding city-wide averages for all purpose-built rentals. Eligible sites must be under 8,000 sq m, have fewer than 10 existing rental units, and not yet have a Stage 1 building permit (with limited exceptions for permits issued Dec 15, 2025 – Mar 20, 2026).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for council decision on April 14, 2026 — if passed, developers with eligible BMR rezoning projects should review the new affordability adjustment options.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver council to consult on new development cost charges
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will direct staff to consult on a new Amenity Cost Charge (ACC) bylaw and Bill 16 updates to density bonus and inclusionary zoning rules. ACCs fund parks, community centres, libraries, and cultural facilities. The density bonus and inclusionary zoning bylaw amendments are due by June 30, 2026, per provincial law. A referral report on those amendments is expected May 5, 2026; the ACC bylaw is expected July 28, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for public consultation opportunities starting mid-April 2026; a development industry workshop is planned for May 2026.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 29-storey mixed-use tower at Cambie and Smithe
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to refer a rezoning application for 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street to a public hearing. minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 16-storey hotel at 888 West 8th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council voted unanimously to approve in principle a rezoning at 888 West 8th Avenue. The property will change from a C-3A (Commercial) zone to a CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) zone. This allows a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor retail. The building's floor space ratio (FSR) — the total floor area compared to the lot size — will increase from 3.0 to 8.1. The maximum height will go from 9.2 metres (30 feet) to 50.6 metres (166 feet). minutes_briefs.title: Council approved new C-2A mixed-use zone to fast-track rental housing
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to create a new commercial district called C-2A that allows rental housing and small hotels in mixed-use buildings. The decision sends the new zoning rules to a public hearing for final approval. Council also instructed city lawyers to prepare updates to several bylaws — including the Zoning and Development By-law, Rental Housing Stock Official Development Plan, Subdivision By-law, Sign By-law, Noise Control By-law, Parking By-law, and the Downtown Eastside/Oppenheimer Official Development Plan — so they all work with the new C-2A zone. This is part of the city's plan to speed up building more rental homes.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved public consultation on new Amenity Cost Charge By-law
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has directed staff to consult with the public and development industry stakeholders on a proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law and related updates to the Zoning and Development By-law. This by-law would allow the city to charge developers fees to help pay for community amenities like parks, childcare, and community centres that are needed because of new development. The consultation will include the public and key industry groups such as the Urban Development Institute (UDI), NAIOP, and the Homebuilders Association Vancouver (HAVN). The motion was adopted on consent on March 31, 2026.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are a developer or property owner in Vancouver, watch for public consultation notices and consider providing input on the proposed by-law.

The property will be rezoned from R3-3 (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. minutes_briefs.title: Council adopted Coastal Flood Management Policy for Vancouver
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has adopted the Coastal Flood Management Policy, which sets out the City's role in planning and managing both structural and non-structural measures to protect against coastal flooding and sea level rise. A proposed amendment to create an intergovernmental task force for the False Creek floodplain was defeated. The policy was approved unanimously by council members present.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Monitor future reports on flood management coordination for False Creek, Burrard Inlet, and Fraser River floodplains.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics
minutes_briefs.source_body:
6. Coastal Flood Management Policy
March 17, 2026

Staff from Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability and Legal Services responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner

THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy,” which articulates the City’s role and supports planning, implementation, and management of structural and non-structural coastal flood management measures in Vancouver.

carried

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Orr

A. THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy.”

B.  THAT Council directs staff to report back on the scope of work, governance options, resource requirements, and timeline necessary for the City to take the steps necessary to convene, or cause to be convened, an intergovernmental and inter-agency task force for the False Creek floodplain, for the purpose of:

1. identifying major publicly owned, utility, transportation, health, and civic assets at risk from coastal flooding and sea level rise;

2. assessing whether a coordinated, area-wide structural flood management approach would be more effective and cost-efficient than fragmented site-by-site measures;

3. identifying the appropriate roles of the City, Province, Government of Canada, First Nations, agencies, utilities, and other major landowners and infrastructure owners, including, but not limited to, Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation in respect of Granville Island, the Department of National Defence and/or the Canadian Coast Guard in respect of the Kitsilano Coast Guard Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada in respect of the False Creek Harbour Authority, PAVCo in respect of B.C. Place, Providence Health Care in respect to St. Paul's Hospital, Vancouver Fraser Port Authority, Vancouver Airport Authority, TransLink, BC Hydro, Creative Energy, and such other affected public or regulated entities as staff may determine;

4. identifying advocacy, research, technical, and partnership work required to support any future funding strategy or senior-government engagement; and

5.         reporting back on options for a fiscally prudent convening and coordination role for the City that does not prejudice future Council decisions on capital commitments.

C.  THAT the report back in B above also assesses whether similar multi-jurisdictional coordination challenges exist in the Burrard Inlet and Fraser River floodplains, or whether differences in land ownership, infrastructure ownership, and distribution of benefits mean that a False Creek-specific approach is warranted.

LOST (Vote No. 11516)
(Councillors Dominato, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague, Zhou and Mayor Sim opposed)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11517) with Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 57.5% residential property tax share for 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted to set the 2026 property tax distribution at 57.5% residential and 42.5% non-residential. This means the Director of Finance must calculate tax rates for all property classes to hit those targets. The decision was adopted on consent, meaning it passed without debate, with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council adopted $2.39B 2026 budget and property tax levy
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved a $2.39 billion operating budget for 2026. To fund this budget, Council also adopted a General Purposes Tax Levy of $1.224 billion. This means the city will collect that amount from property taxes to cover general city services, debts, and obligations for the year. minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $89,000 in community sport event grants
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved $89,000 in grants for 19 community sport events as part of the Spring 2026 intake of the Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program. The money comes from the 2026 program budget. Each organization must sign a grant agreement letter before receiving the funds. Council also officially recognized the non-charity organizations on the list as contributing to the city's culture, health, or welfare, which is a legal step required under the Vancouver Charter.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
3. Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
March 10, 2026

A. THAT Council approve 19 new Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants totalling $89,000 as outlined in the body of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” to each organization listed in Table 1 of the same report, under the column entitled “Organization” and for the amount set out beside their name in the column entitled “Grant Recommended”; source of funding is the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget.

B. THAT the grants in A above be subject to each grant recipient agreeing to the terms and conditions of the community sport hosting grant program as generally outlined in this report and otherwise satisfactory to the Director, Tourism and Destination Events and the City Solicitor.  

C. THAT no legal rights or obligations are created by the approval of the A and B above unless and until the grant agreement letter is executed and delivered by the grant recipient.  

D. THAT pursuant to the Section 206(1)(i) of the Vancouver Charter, the organizations listed in Table 1 of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” which are not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency are deemed by Council to be an organization contributing to the culture, beautification, health, or welfare of the City.  

ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND A BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11523)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $9.6M in 2026 cultural grants with amendments
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved $9.6 million in 2026 cultural grants, including operating funding for institutions, annual grants, and multi-year grants for 83 cultural organizations. Council also approved additional funding: $25,000 from reserves for the Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society, $75,000 for the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society's feasibility study, and a one-time $728,000 grant to Historic Joy Kogawa House for hazardous materials removal and accessibility upgrades. The decision was made at the March 30, 2026 council meeting.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are one of the listed grant recipients, contact the City's Arts, Culture and Community Services department to finalize your grant agreement.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved call for senior government homelessness funding
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council voted unanimously to receive the 2025 Homeless Count report and to formally call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness. Council also asks senior governments to re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre, expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across Metro Vancouver and BC, strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness, and provide support to local governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports.
minutes_briefs.industries: Healthcare, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body:
1. 2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
April 1, 2026

The General Manager, Arts, Culture and Community Services along with staff from Arts, Culture and Community Services, provided a presentation and responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Dominato
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver” for information.

B. THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to:  

a. Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness;

b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre;  

c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access close to home and community;  

d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness; and  

e. Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering.  

amended 

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Maloney

THAT the words “and housing with supports” be added to the end of e.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11512)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11513) with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED

A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver” for information.

B. THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to:  

a. Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness;

b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre;  

c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access close to home and community;  

d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness; and  

e. Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports.
